content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies Like CHEF?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** It's such a cool straightforward movie. There's no real antagonist or plot twist. He lost his job in the beginning, but that was more of a setup to the story. He wanted to do his own thing, and he did. Taking his son along and just having an awesome experience without conflict.

Are there other movies like that? where its just good vibes the whole time?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. World's Fastest Indian 
  - This is a good movie
2. Peanut butter falcon.
3. Nonnas
4. The Straight Story
5. Local Hero, Comfort and Joy, or the very old one, You Can't Take It With You
6. I love Chef. Zero stakes. Just a nice time.
7. Big Night

It's also centered around a chef. No real antagonist, simple plot, it's funny, it's heartwarming, it has really great actors giving superb performances and portraying lovable characters. It's definitely not as uplifting as Chef, but honestly I think it's perfect.
8. Beautiful Girls
9. bob trevino likes it

## 2. Psychological thriller suggestions

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I’m looking for psychological thriller movie recommendations that genuinely mess with your head and stay with you long after the credits roll.

I’m not just looking for jump scares or basic twists — I want something intense, layered, and mind-bending. Movies that make you question reality, human psychology, morality, or what actually happened. Slow burn is fine as long as the payoff is worth it.

I enjoy films with:

	•	Unreliable narrators

	•	Dark themes

	•	Shocking or unsettling twists

	•	Strong atmosphere and storytelling

Language or country doesn’t matter — Hollywood, Korean, European, indie, anything is welcome.

Basically, I want a movie that makes me sit in silence afterward thinking, “What did I just watch?”

Drop your best recommendations 👀🍿

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chinatown (1974)

Angel Heart (1987)

The Hot Spot (1990)

The Last Seduction (1994)

Devil in a Blue Dress (1995)

The Usual Suspects (1995)

L.A. Confidential (1997)

The Crimson Rivers (France 2000)

Infernal Affairs (Hong Kong 2002), remade as The Departed (2006)

The director's cut of The Butterfly Effect (2004)

The original version of The Secret in Their Eyes (Argentina 2009)

The Red Riding trilogy: The Year of Our Lord 1974/1980/1983 (Britain 2009)

Shutter Island (2010)

Marshland (Spain 2014)

Canola (Korea 2016)
2. Mulholland Drive
3. Strange Darling (2023)

Go in blind.
4. Some movies that come to mind - might not be perfect matches though.

Nightcrawler (2014)
Vanilla Sky (2001)
Gone Girl (2014)
  - Second Nightcrawler.
5. The Machinist

Session 9

The Jacket
6. Oldboy 

Requiem for a Dream

Shutter Island

Annihilation
7. Mulholland Drive (2001)

Lost Highway (1997)
8. Always good to look at the classics:

Rebecca

Vertigo

Psycho

The Thing

The 39 Steps

Diabolique (French movie)
9. The Wailing. 

It’s a Korean supernatural horror but left me mind fucked for days. It is however 2.5 hours long so avoid drinking Coke or Monster before hand.

## 3. Any dark/psychological thrillers movies?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Honestly, if you suggest it I probably haven't watched it because I'm still trying to get into movies so please suggest anything!

(Edit: TYSM guys these are going on my movie list love yall)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Primal Fear

Prisoners

The Machinist

The Vanishing (1988)

Se7en

We Need to Talk About Kevin
2. Everyone will downvote me for this but I like *malice*
  - I loved Malice
3. Destroyer

Pacific Heights
  - I will never be a landlord after watching Pacific Heights.
4. Marathon Man (1976)
5. Shutter Island

Carry On

Mystic River

Nightcrawler

Sixth Sense

Zodiac
6. Mulholland Drive (2001) and Lost Highway (1997).
7. Dead Calm.
Unlawful Entry.
8. Primal Fear

## 4. Movies that take place in a gas station that arent comedy

**Score:** 12
**Body:** I've seen many suggestions that lean more into the comedy side, I don't like the genre. Looking for more eerie or melancholy type of vibe/genre.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Maximum Overdrive" / "Trucks"

Almost the whole thing takes place in a truckstop in the middle of nowhere.
2. Splinter (2008)
3. Last Stop in Yuma County
4. No Country for Old Men has a pretty unsettling gas station scene.
5. Legion 2010 might be close enough / have the right vibe for you
6. I'm remembering that "The Stand" had a pretty influential gas station scene but I can't remember if that was in the book or movie/miniseries...
7. Maximum Overdrive(1985)
8. The Vanishing
9. Open 24 hours
10. Bagdad cafe

## 5. Films like the beginning of Stargate

**Score:** 12
**Body:** The first two acts of Stargate are among my favorite bits of cinema ever. I really like the idea of people exploring things, either extraterrestrial or supernatural, that nobody has ever explored before. Sorta fish out of water, but also there’s someone who is an expert and is discovering things that are surprisingly familiar.

Are there other movies like this? I know Arrival is very similar in that Amy Adams’ character is a linguist >!who is eventually able to translate the aliens’ language.!<

Something like that. But also the whole idea of the terrifying experience of diving head first into the unknown.

Also have enjoyed (as mentioned below): Stalker, Annihilation, Contact, Close Encounters, Fifth Element, Galaxy Quest, Interstellar, Event Horizon

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There is a show called Scavengers Reign you might enjoy 
  - Scavengers Reign is fucking awesome
2. Close Encounters of the Third Kind. 
Fifth Element.  
3. Contact ; The Abyss ; Cocoon ;2010 the year we make contact; The man who fell to Earth; Farscape ;Alien Encounters
4. Contact. from the amazing opening shot to the discovery of the signal to the building of the machine… it’s exhilarating. it falls apart at the end for me, but your mileage may vary.
5. Annihilation 
6. Annihilation
7. As someone who loves everything about Stargate... That specific combination is what makes the film so great. If you have not watched Stargate SG-1 (TV Series 1997–2007) and Stargate: Atlantis (TV Series 2004–2009)... Start there.

Here is a list of films and shows that share similar attributes. Science fiction, discovery, exploration, adventure, mystery, scientific or historical knowledge, etc.

  
Epoch (2001) & Epoch: Evolution (2003) \[Basically the original Arrival}

The Abyss (1989)

Contact (1997)

Sphere (1998)

Moonfall (2022)

Travelers (TV Series 2016–2018) \[also by Brad Wright\]

The Expanse (TV Series 2015–2022)

Farscape (TV Series 1999–2003) \[Ben Browder & Claudia Black from later SG1\]

Doctor Who (TV Series 2005–2022)

Battlestar Galactica (TV Series 2004–2009)

Dark Matter (TV Series 2015–2017)

Lost in Space (TV Series 2018–2021)

Star Trek: Enterprise (TV Series 2001–2005)

The Librarians (TV Series 2014–2018)

Continuum (TV Series 2012–2015)

Haven (TV Series 2010–2015) \[Based off of Stephen King's "The Colorado Kid"\]
8. The Last  Starfighter, Green Lantern
9. Interstellar

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. In "A Clockwork Orange" if Alex wasn't too hard with his droogs. Would he still get betrayed after the catlady incident?

**Score:** 9

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Or were his droogs planning to betray him?
  - I think his droogs acted purely because of how he treated them not because of Alex’s moral standpoint
    - Oh so they were just as insane as him then
      - Haha one could say
2. She probably kidnapped herself.
  - She owes money all over town, to known pornagraphers... which is cool man.
    - New information has come to light man
      - I am The Walrus
3. he's an anarchist, they're bourgeois capitalists (and dim), what do you think?

## 2. Favorite movie of the year 2025

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I laughed. Comedies are an endangered species in films. The rest of the critically acclaimed stuff is hot garbage. This at least was fun. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I may be old.   Ethan Suplee is the only name I recognized.   Thanks for sharing!  I will check it out. 

Happy new year!
  - Stavvy is becoming an icon. Deservedly so i think
    - He was hilarious in Bugonia
      - I love Stavvy.  He IS hilarious.  Wouldn’t say he was hilarious in this, though.

## 3. The biggest cinematic dud of the year for me - with the cringiest line deliveries that almost made it seem comedic in how badly the characters were written

**Score:** 0
**Body:** https://preview.redd.it/jacklfdzfnag1.jpg?width=1080&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=21cabff8eb922a280c703254d00da6630c5f0dc9

I love PTA and almost every movie he has made. This one, I was stunned at how mediocre it was. The ""revolutionaries" were so cringey, written like a high schooler's idea of what political dialog sounds like. DiCaprio delivered this line like a corny afterthought that reminded me of the South Park movie. The story is basically just "a dad will do anything to get his daughter back and oh ya there's some super ambiguous, shapeless political stuff in there too" and it was treated basically as like just a backdrop. No complexity in the characters, or exploration of them. The trailer made it seem like a sweeping epic across decades, when it wasn't even close to that complex of a film. 

It wasn't the worst film I saw this year, but definitely the most disappointing one I have seen in a really long time. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The political backdrop was pretty caricatured  unfortunately and did lack some authenticity
  - Identity politics THEMSELVES lack authenticity.\
That was one of the points, a MAJOR one.
2. On so much of the stuff you’d actually think was good this year the writing was absolutely garbage. The new one about whistle blowers was bad too.
3. Agreed. Major disappointment. Especially given all the raving about it. I'm sure it'll probably win awards. Snooze.
4. Lame take. Seems like folks just don't get satire any more.
5. I didn’t hate it, but I do agree with this for the most part. DiCaprio’s character was pretty cringe.
6. “Almost made it seem comedic”

Boy, do I have something to tell you…

Edit: coming back after reading the comments. I didn’t think everyone here was going to miss the point and tone and depth of the movie. But unfortunately that appears to be the case. I’m not saying has to like OBAA, but there’s a difference between not liking it and not getting it and thinking you’re better than the movie. And a lot of people here are doing the latter.
  - Okay so what you're saying is that Paul Thomas Anderson intentionally made a film mocking and lampooning leftist American politics in the year 2025?

I don't think this is the saving grace of a comment that you think it is, and that you lack nuance in understanding which of the elements of this film were satirical and which ones were just poorly written.

In the event that he was lampooning both sides of the spectrum, why even make the film if the most you can muster as far as political commentary is "hmm both sides bad." Again, that's a teenagers understanding of the world. If you are going to do that, you need to show earnest moments from each side that pump the breaks on the satire and give the audience a view into their earnest rationale. Doing this would've more successfully made the film what it was billed as - a genre bending epic. Which it wasn't. What you and everyone else here saying it was pure satire is saying is that the movie was just some long, useless skit. Which it actually was. But I can't fathom why you think A) the satire was intelligent or B) why you believe it was an important film.

---
